topic: Someone finds out their medical records were mixed up with another patient's
raw_output:
[story 1]

The printed summary lay crumpled on the sterile white desk, a stark, undeniable proof of incompetence. My hands were shaking so badly I had to grip the edge of the laminate surface to steady myself. It was my file, the one detailing my recent diagnostics, but the diagnoses listed belonged to some stranger—a man named Mr. Henderson, based on the name on the referral slip. I reread the section detailing the surgical notes, the specific drug regimens. A cold, tight knot began to form in my stomach, tightening with every line of text. I slammed the folder shut, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet examination room. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small space, my jaw clenched so tightly my molars ached. I needed to find someone who could explain this mess, but the thought of confronting the administrative staff made my skin crawl. Every glance from a passing nurse felt like an accusation.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the glowing text mocking me with its misplaced details. My breath hitched, shallow and rapid. It was impossible. My blood felt suddenly icy, pooling in my extremities. I leaned closer, forcing my eyes to absorb the discrepancy—the dates, the specific test results that simply didn't align with my own history. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat, a sound of pure, contained fury. I pushed back from the desk, the chair scraping harshly against the linoleum floor. My knuckles were white where I gripped my forearm. "This is a mistake," I managed to hiss, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. I didn't wait for a response; I just turned and walked toward the exit, every step deliberate and sharp, radiating a palpable sense of deep displeasure.

[story 3]

The envelope was a physical assault. It was my paperwork, yet the information inside belonged to someone else entirely, a complete fabrication of my medical reality. I held it between two fingers, the paper crinkling under the pressure. My gaze swept across the clinic lobby, landing on the receptionist who was calmly filing charts. I felt a sudden, intense heat rising in my chest, a pressure building behind my sternum that demanded release. I walked directly toward the counter, my gait stiff and unnaturally controlled. When I finally spoke, my voice was dangerously level, stripped bare of any warmth. "I need to see the supervisor immediately," I stated, my eyes locked onto hers, conveying a clear message that this situation was not merely inconvenient, but deeply offensive.
